intro.title: CONTRACTS, NOT CONVENTIONS.
intro.description: The Zenith contract is explicit at author time, visible in output, and enforced before the browser has to guess.

conventions.title: Starter Page Composition
conventions.meaning: The starter template composes a page through DefaultLayout.
conventions.description: This markup block comes straight from the current basic starter page. It uses real Zenith page composition, canonical on:click binding, and slot-driven layout usage instead of invented shell components.
conventions.snippet:
Zenith Basic Starter

Start with a single route, a minimal layout, and plain global CSS.

Add routes later under src/pages.

conventions.title: State-Driven Markup
conventions.meaning: Use state when a value directly drives DOM output.
conventions.description: The canonical reactivity docs use state for DOM-facing updates and pair it with a function-valued on:click handler. The syntax here matches current Zenith documentation exactly.
conventions.snippet:
<script lang="ts">
state open = false
function toggle() { open = !open }
<\/script>

<button on:click={toggle}>{open ? "Open" : "Closed"}</button>

conventions.title: Signal Updates
conventions.meaning: signal() uses explicit get/set updates with stable identity.
conventions.description: The current reactivity docs define signal() with explicit get() and set() calls. This is the canonical low-level update shape for frequent or cross-boundary values.

conventions.title: Layout Slot Surface
conventions.meaning: Layouts expose real structure through a literal <slot />.
conventions.description: The basic starter DefaultLayout is authored as plain Zenith markup with a real slot outlet. It shows the structural surface the page composes into, not a fabricated runtime wrapper.
conventions.snippet: Zenith Basic Starter

conventions.title: Function-Valued Events
conventions.meaning: Component APIs forward function props into canonical DOM bindings.
conventions.description: The current events docs show component handlers transported as function references, then wired back into DOM markup through on:click. This is the canonical event-forwarding pattern in Zenith today.
conventions.snippet:
<script lang="ts">
interface Props {
  onClick: () => void;
}

const incoming = props as Props;
<\/script>

<button on:click={incoming.onClick}>Increment</button>
